The Optimal
Measure of
Distinctiveness

Aligning Theory of Evaluation
with Practice of Measurement

Aria Huang University of Oxford
Eric Zhao University of Oxford
Alexander Vossen Tilburg University
Today's Seminar

Five Parts

  1. 01Motivation & Research Question — Why does measurement matter for OD?
  2. 02Structured Review — How have 129 studies measured distinctiveness?
  3. 03Empirical Comparison — Do 42 different measures yield different results?
  4. 04Theory–Method Alignment Framework — Five steps to "optimal" measurement
  5. 05Discussion & Implications — Theory, methods, practice
01
Part One

Motivation &
Research Question

Why does measurement choice matter for optimal distinctiveness?

Motivation

What Does It Mean to Be Distinctive?

Distinctiveness lies at the core of strategy and organization research — but it does not exist in a vacuum. It must be seen and recognized by audiences.

Innovation Fleming, 2001
Competitive Positioning Porter, 1985, 1996
Legitimacy DiMaggio & Powell, 1983
Organizational Identity Albert & Whetten, 1985
Motivation

A Thriving Field: Optimal Distinctiveness

Organizations must balance conformity and differentiation to achieve favorable outcomes.

Conceptual Advances
  • —Multidimensionality (Bu et al., 2022)
  • —Contextual contingencies (Haans, 2019)
  • —Reference benchmarks (Majzoubi et al., 2025)
  • —Temporal dynamics (Zhao et al., 2018)
Methodological Advances
  • —Morphing technology (Bu et al., 2022)
  • —QCA (McKnight & Zietsma, 2018)
  • —NLP & Machine Learning (Majzoubi & Zhao, 2023)
Motivation · The Puzzle

Yet Findings Are Empirically Fragmented

Studies asking the same question — how distinctiveness relates to performance — arrive at divergent conclusions.

Inverted-U
Moderate distinctiveness
is optimal
Majzoubi & Zhao, 2023; Haans, 2019;
Phillips et al., 2025; Taeuscher & Rothe, 2021; Zhao et al., 2018
U-Shaped
Extremes outperform
moderate positions
van Angeren et al., 2022;
Taeuscher et al., 2022
Linear
More / less distinctiveness
monotonically better or worse
Barlow et al., 2019; Majzoubi et al., 2025;
Taeuscher et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2020
"The problem is not
weak theory.
It is misalignment between
theory and measurement."

Measurement Conventionalism — Popper, 1959

Theory of Evaluation
How audiences perceive & value difference
Practice of Measurement
Proxy · Reference · Metric choices
Research Question

How should distinctiveness be measured so that empirical indicators reflect the evaluative processes theory claims to describe?

Step 01
Structured Review
129 empirical OD studies across 32 journals (1990–2026)
Step 02
Empirical Comparison
Apply 42 measures to 88,093 children's books on Goodreads
Step 03
Alignment Framework
Five-step theory–method alignment linking evaluation theory to measurement practice
02
Part Two

Structured
Review

How have 129 OD studies operationalized distinctiveness — and what does that variation reveal?

Structured Review

Review Design & Sample

  • Search: Scopus · distinct*, unique*, similar*, atypical*, differen*, typical*
  • Method: Three-stage — keyword search + citation tracing → LLM screening → manual full-text review
  • Scope: FT50 (anchored at Deephouse, 1999) expanded to include AJS, ASR, and adjacent journals
  • Final sample: 129 studies · 1990–2026 · 23 journals
  • Principle: Theoretical alignment — congruence between conceptual definition and operationalization
Journal Distribution · N = 129 papers
Structured Review

Measurement Choices Encode Evaluative Theories

Data Proxies
What cues are encoded?
  • Category membership
  • Text / Narrative
  • Strategic configuration
  • Physical / Visual features
Reference Models
Against what baseline?
  • Competitor (pairwise)
  • Prototype (centroid)
  • Exemplar (salient cases)
Competitor & Prototype dominate; Exemplar underused
Metrics
How is difference quantified?
  • Vector-based: Manhattan · Euclidean · Cosine · Mahalanobis
  • Set-based: Jaccard · Dice
  • Distributional: Jensen-Shannon Divergence
96% use established metrics; remainder use customized variants
Structured Review · 129 Studies

How the Field Measures Distinctiveness

Proxy Type — N=131 excl. Unknown
Metric — N=105 excl. Others
Reference / Anchor — N=105 excl. Others
Structured Review · Synthesis

What the Review Tells Us

  • Measurement choices encode evaluative theories — Audience + Proxy + Reference + Metric = an implicit, often unexamined account of how audiences judge difference
  • Measurement heterogeneity is patterned, not idiosyncratic — it mirrors the heterogeneity of underlying evaluative logics across 129 studies
  • Four systematic blind spots — single-audience assumption, reference default, equal weighting, metric fungibility — each producing structured, not random, fragmentation

But does measurement choice actually move empirical results? Let us show you.

03
Part Three

Empirical
Comparison

42 measures applied to one dataset. Same data, same controls, same estimation — only the measure changes.

Empirical Comparison

Design Logic & Data

Core strategy: Hold everything constant except the measure.

Same dataset · Same controls · Same estimation (OLS + robust SEs) · Vary only the measure

  • Dataset: 88,093 children's books from Goodreads.com (English, 2007–2017) — largest social cataloguing platform with 60M+ users
  • Why children's books? Key cues (genre tags, descriptions) are externally visible, consistently structured, and audiences are well-defined
  • Outcomes: (1) Average Goodreads rating — perceived quality  ·  (2) Ratings count — popularity/reach
  • Scale: 42 measures × 3 model specs × 2 outcomes = 252 total models
Empirical Comparison

Constructing 42 Measures

2proxies × 3reference models × 7metrics = 42distinctiveness measures
Proxy 1 · Topical
Narrative / Text
LDA topic model on book descriptions (60 topics, selected via coherence & perplexity optimization). Each book = 60-dimensional probability vector on a simplex.
Compositional geometry: different metrics capture similar probability-mass movements → expect convergence
Proxy 2 · Categorical
Genre Membership
9 sub-genre binary tags (10 total; "children's book" is the population genre). Each book = sparse binary vector.
Discrete geometry: metric & reference interact to change meaning → expect divergence
7 Metrics: Manhattan · Euclidean · Cosine · Mahalanobis · Jaccard · Dice · Jensen-Shannon Divergence
Empirical Comparison · Result 1

Rank-Order Agreement Across 42 Measures

  • Topical measures: Highly correlated regardless of metric or reference — shared representational geometry on the probability simplex
  • Categorical measures: Moderate to low cross-metric correlations — set-based and vector-based metrics align only partially
  • Cross-proxy correlations: Mean Spearman ρ ≈ 0.14 — different proxies rank books very differently

Proxy choice accounts for the largest drop in alignment.

Metrics matter less within a proxy family. Proxies matter most across families. Two proxies identify different books as distinctively positioned.

Empirical Comparison · Result 2

Topical Converges. Categorical Diverges.

Topical Distinctiveness (all 7 metrics)
Consistent Inverted-U
All metrics yield similar functional forms — high convergence across metrics and reference models.
In the probability simplex, different metrics capture similar movements of probability mass. Convergence reflects shared representational geometry.
Categorical Distinctiveness (same data)
Three Different Forms
Mahalanobis×Prototype → inverted-U  ·  Mahalanobis×Competitor → changes  ·  Jaccard×Competitor → monotonic
Identical data can support either optimal distinctiveness or linear differentiation — depending solely on measurement choices.
"Methodological variance
mirrors theoretical variance."

Where the underlying evaluative logic is unified, measures converge.
Where multiple mechanisms are plausible, measures diverge.

Measurement is not a technical footnote. It is an act of theorizing.

04
Part Four

Theory–Method
Alignment
Framework

Five sequential steps that link the logic of audience evaluation to concrete measurement choices.

Framework

Five Steps to Theoretically Aligned Measurement

STEP 01
Audience
Who evaluates?
Audience specification constrains all downstream choices
STEP 02
Cues
What is observed?
Which signals are diagnostic in this evaluative context?
STEP 03
Representation
How is it encoded?
Sets · Vectors · Distributions
STEP 04
Reference
Compared to whom?
Competitor · Prototype · Exemplar
STEP 05
Commensuration
How is it quantified?
The metric encodes a cognitive algebra

Grounded in Information Integration Theory (Anderson, 1974)

Framework · Step 1

Audience — Who Structures the Evaluation?

Audience variation is not a moderator — it structurally constrains the entire evaluation process.

  • Attentional capacity → constrains cue selection  ·  Consumers: salient categorical cues; critics: nuanced stylistic elements
  • Cognitive construal → constrains representation  ·  Coarse categorical schemas vs. fine-grained continuous construals
  • Evaluative priorities → constrains commensuration  ·  Ranking platforms: additive transparency; VCs: risk/covariance logic; regulators: categorical compliance
Framework · Steps 2 & 3

Cues & Representation

Step 02 — Cues
  • —Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic (capabilities vs. labels, endorsements)
  • —Cue diagnosticity: not all signals are equally informative — varies by evaluative context
  • —Cue selection determines what differences are visible at all
Step 03 — Representation
  • —Set-based (Jaccard/Dice) — binary membership; high construal, categorical audiences
  • —Vector-based (Euclidean/Cosine/Mahalanobis) — continuous attributes; feature-sensitive, expert audiences
  • —Distributional (JSD) — probabilistic profiles; uncertainty-sensitive evaluation
Framework · Step 4

Reference — Distinctiveness from Whom?

Competitor
Exhaustive search

Pairwise deviation from all peers. Plausible in transparent, competitive markets with low search costs.

Typical: Analysts · Acquirers · Strategic audiences

Prototype
Efficient search

Deviation from the category centroid. Plausible in mature, institutionalized categories with stable norms.

Typical: Mass consumers · Generalist audiences

Exemplar
Heuristic search

Deviation from salient, high-status cases. Plausible in nascent markets or contexts with clear status hierarchies.

Typical: Consumers · Novices · Status-aware audiences

Framework · Step 5

Commensuration — How Are Comparisons Quantified?

"The social process of transforming qualitative differences into a common quantitative metric" — Espeland & Stevens, 1998

Logic Metrics Evaluative Assumption
MagnitudeManhattan, EuclideanTotal deviation accumulates; many small differences sum like one large one
DirectionalCosinePattern alignment matters more than scale of difference
Variance-weightedMahalanobisRarity is diagnostic; rare deviations are amplified
OverlapJaccard, DicePresence or absence of shared membership is key
DistributionalJSDDivergence in probabilistic profiles under uncertainty

The metric determines not only how distinctiveness is measured, but what distinctiveness means.

Framework · Integration

The Steps Are Sequential but Interdependent

  • Audience specification → constrains cue selection and weighting
  • Cues → shape plausible representational geometries
  • Representations → limit feasible reference models and metrics
  • Reference + Commensuration → jointly determine the meaning of deviation

The framework does not prescribe a single optimal measure.

It specifies constraints: for any given theory and context, only a subset of measurement choices are theoretically defensible.

Framework · Illustration

Applying the Framework — Two Audiences, Two Designs

Step
Goodreads Consumers
Investors / Analysts
Audience
Aggregated platform ratings — broad, consumer-driven
Covariance-sensitive, risk-focused, high cognitive capacity
Cues
Genre tags + book descriptions — externally visible
Financial metrics, strategic positioning signals
Representation
Vector-based (topic proportions)
Vector-based with covariance structure
Reference
Prototype — deviation from genre centroid
Competitor — exhaustive pairwise comparison
Metric
Manhattan / Euclidean — magnitude logic
Mahalanobis — rarity is diagnostic

Each choice flows from the audience's evaluative logic — not methodological convention.

05
Part Five

Discussion &
Implications

What does this mean for OD theory, for methods, and for practice?

Discussion

Contributions to OD Theory

01
Explains Empirical Fragmentation
Inconsistencies across 129 studies driven by implicit measurement assumptions, not weak theory. Our review and empirical comparison make this explicit and interpretable.
02
Principled Basis for Measurement Choice
Five-step framework helps researchers select measures consistent with their theoretical claims — directly resolving the four pervasive blind spots.
03
Measurement as a Theoretical Act
Transforms methodological diversity from a source of fragmentation into a foundation for cumulative theorizing. Optimality is not a fixed threshold — it is audience- and context-contingent.
Discussion · Meta-Theoretical Contribution

Beyond OD: A Generalizable Framework

Many core constructs share the relational structure of distinctiveness — they exist as judgments formed by audiences comparing entities against one another and against shared expectations.

Novelty
How new is this relative to what existed before?
Legitimacy
How appropriate is this relative to audience expectations?
Identity
How coherent is this relative to category norms?
Status
How positioned is this relative to a hierarchy?
Atypicality
How unusual is this combination of features?
Common thread
When measurement does not specify whose evaluation is being modeled, apparent fragmentation may reflect unarticulated measurement assumptions — not substantive theory.
Discussion · Practical Implications

For Practitioners: Ask a Better Question

Distinctiveness is not a fixed attribute — it is an audience-contingent judgment. Move beyond "How different are we?" to:

"Different according to whom, and relative to what?"

01
Identify which cues your target audience can observe and prioritize — investors attend to financials; consumers to labels and narratives; regulators to compliance
02
Understand how audiences compare alternatives — against the prototype? against exemplars? against all competitors?
03
Match your differentiation strategy to the audience's integration rule — additive for ranking platforms; rarity-sensitive for VC investors
Discussion · Limitations & Future Research

Limitations & Future Directions

  • Empirical scope: Single context (Goodreads). Future work should validate against theoretically specified audiences across domains with known evaluative logics
  • Static measurement: Distinctiveness co-evolves with audiences, reference points, and category boundaries — longitudinal designs that model prototype drift are needed
  • Logics plurality: Institutional settings may contain competing evaluative structures within one audience field (Thornton et al., 2012)
  • Advanced metrics: Audience-specific learned distance functions and embedding-based similarity — validated against actual evaluator judgments — remain underexplored
"Measuring distinctiveness is not
a neutral act of quantification.
It is an implicit theory
of how audiences perceive
and value difference."
Summary

The Framework Reframes Measurement as a Theoretical Act

Step 01 Audience Who structures the evaluation? Constrains all downstream choices.
Step 02 Cues What constitutes distinctiveness? Diagnosticity varies by context.
Step 03 Representation How are cues encoded? Sets · Vectors · Distributions.
Step 04 Reference Compared to whom? Competitor · Prototype · Exemplar.
Step 05 Commensuration How are comparisons quantified? The metric encodes a cognitive algebra.

When measurement aligns with theory, methodological diversity becomes cumulative theorizing — not fragmentation.

Thank You
Closing
"The yardstick you choose
defines what you will find."

Distinctiveness is not merely something organizations possess or researchers measure — it is a lens through which theory itself is enacted.

Aria Huang aria.huang.dphil@said.oxford.edu
Eric Zhao eric.zhao@sbs.ox.ac.uk
Alexander Vossen a.vossen@tilburguniversity.edu

Appendix C

Review
Methodology

How the structured review was conducted
Appendix C1 · Review Methodology

Search Strategy & Scope

Database

Scopus — title, abstract, and keywords

Search String

distinct* OR unique* OR similar* OR
atypical* OR differen* OR typical*
 + distinctiveness / optimal distinctiveness

Journal Scope

FT50 journals (anchored at Deephouse, 1999) +
American Journal of Sociology, American Sociological Review,
and adjacent strategy & sociology outlets

1990
Earliest year in sample
2026
Latest year in sample
23
Journals represented
~3,400
Records before deduplication
Appendix C2 · Review Methodology

Three-Stage Screening Process

Stage 1
~3,400
Keyword Search + Citation Tracing
Scopus keyword query across FT50 + AJS/ASR journals · supplemented by backward citation tracing from key seed papers (Deephouse 1999; Hannan et al. 2007)
↓
~320
LLM-Assisted Title & Abstract Screening
Claude Opus used to screen each record for relevance: must (a) study organizational identity, positioning, or market differentiation, and (b) include or discuss an empirical measure of distinctiveness or similarity
↓
129
Manual Full-Text Review → Final Sample
Authors independently read each candidate in full · included only if the paper reports a concrete operationalization of distinctiveness or similarity as an independent or dependent variable
Appendix C3 · Review Methodology

Coding Protocol

Each paper was coded on four primary measurement dimensions:

01 · Proxy Type
Semantic / Categorical / Strategic-Configurational / Physical
02 · Representation
Vector / distribution / set (e.g. TF-IDF, topic proportions, IPC class shares)
03 · Metric
Cosine / Manhattan / Euclidean / Jaccard / Mahalanobis / Dice / Jensen–Shannon
04 · Reference Model
Competitor-based / Prototype (centroid) / Exemplar (salient peer)

Secondary coding dimensions:

  • — Dependent variable: ratings, survival, prices, growth, status
  • — Industry context: technology, cultural, financial, pharma, services
  • — Explicit OD theory: does the paper cite Deephouse / Zuckerman / Hannan?
  • — Robustness checks: does the paper test multiple metrics or proxies?
Appendix D

Reference
Tables

Proxies · Reference Models · Distance Metrics
Appendix D1 · Reference Tables

Summary of Common Data Proxies

Proxy Type Description Theoretical Background Example Applications Exemplar Papers
Semantic / Symbolic Distinctiveness as deviation in meaning, framing, or discursive content of organizational self-presentations; symbolic boundaries through language. Cultural entrepreneurship & cultural sociology; narrative composition & storytelling shape evaluative legitimacy. Topic-model distributions, word embeddings of 10-Ks, IPO prospectuses, crowdfunding narratives, patent texts. Haans (2019); Taeuscher et al. (2021, 2022); Gouvard et al. (2026); Arts et al. (2025)
Categorical Relative position within classification systems and category boundaries; conformity vs. deviance from recognized categories. Category theory; boundary spanning, legitimacy, and evaluation hinging on categorical belonging. Patent-class distributions (IPC/USPC/CPC), genre-tag memberships, SIC/NAICS codes, drug therapeutic-area portfolios. Cutolo & Ferriani (2024); Janisch & Vossen (2022); Taeuscher et al. (2021)
Strategic Configurational Deviation from industry-average configurations of resource allocations, capabilities, and activity systems; competitive advantage. Resource-based view & competitive strategy; configurational differentiation rather than symbolic interpretation. R&D and advertising intensity ratios, asset-allocation profiles, CSR emphasis vectors, sales distributions across segments. Gimeno & Woo (1996); Deephouse (1999); Girardi et al. (2021)
Physical Variation in directly apprehensible physical, visual, or sonic attributes of the offering or artifact itself. Aesthetic economy & design-based theories; material and visual turn in organization studies. Automobile frontal-design geometry, CNN image embeddings, audio feature vectors, color-space coordinates, molecular fingerprints. Bu et al. (2022); Fuchs et al. (2023); Banerjee et al. (2023)
Appendix D2 · Reference Tables

Summary of Common Reference Models

Reference Model Description Suitable Context Exemplar Papers
Competitor Measures distinctiveness through direct pairwise comparisons among all category members — relative positioning within a competitive field. Most useful in transparent, densely competitive markets where evaluators can maintain a mental map of many rivals; cognitively costly in opaque or rapidly changing contexts. Arts & Vossen (2023); Carlson et al. (2023); Hoberg & Phillips (2016); Giorgi et al. (2015); Lo & Kenney (2015)
Prototype Measures distinctiveness as deviation from the centroid of a category — evaluated relative to a generalized category standard highlighting typicality. Works best in mature, institutionalized categories with stable central norms; less useful in nascent or fragmented fields. Taeuscher et al. (2022, 2021); Gouvard et al. (2026); Haans (2019); Barlow et al. (2019); Hanisch et al. (2024)
Exemplar Measures distinctiveness relative to the most salient or successful category members — divergence from highly recognized, exemplary cases rather than average norms. Most plausible in nascent markets, high-status hierarchies, or information-scarce contexts where a few iconic cases dominate attention. Zhao et al. (2018); Barlow et al. (2019); Khessina et al. (2016)
Appendix D3 · Reference Tables

Common Distance Metrics

Metric Formula Description Exemplar Papers
Manhattan Distance ∑|xᵢ − yᵢ| Absolute distance between two points across all dimensions; robust to outliers. Shen et al. (2024); Taeuscher et al. (2021); Zhang et al. (2020); Haans (2019); Cennamo (2013)
Cosine Distance 1 − (A·B / ‖A‖‖B‖) Angle-based distance between vectors; scale-invariant, preferred for high-dimensional text. Banerjee et al. (2023); Janisch & Vossen (2022); Barlow et al. (2019); Hoberg & Phillips (2016)
Euclidean Distance √∑(aᵢ − bᵢ)² Straight-line distance; sensitive to scale and dimensionality. Bu et al. (2022); Zhao et al. (2018); Litov et al. (2012); Gimeno & Woo (1996)
Jaccard Distance 1 − |A∩B| / |A∪B| Set-based non-overlap; natural for binary or categorical feature spaces. Cutolo & Ferriani (2024); Chan et al. (2021); Arts et al. (2018)
Dice Distance 1 − 2|A∩B| / (|A|+|B|) Set overlap with harmonic denominator; penalizes large sets more than Jaccard. Smith & Chae (2016); Lo & Kenney (2015)
Mahalanobis Distance √(x−y)ᵀ S(x−y) Accounts for correlations among dimensions via covariance matrix S; distribution-aware. Claes & Vissa (2020); Kim & Rhee (2017)
Jensen–Shannon Divergence ½D(A‖M) + ½D(B‖M) Symmetric KL divergence between probability distributions; M = average of A and B. Majoubi & Zhao (2023)
